---
title: Workshop on Faith / Dignity / Work
draft: true
---




1. **We are only discussing what happens if AI fails.** We are talking about what happens if the elevator stalls.

2. **Concrete vision: 7B people in apartment buildings,** can order whatever they want, don't need to do anything.

3. **Historical precedents for post-scarcity.** UAE; religious scholars; long-term unemployed; aristocrats;

4. **Values are absorbed from peers; and in turn downstream of the economic situation.**
    - Individual values are half biological, half cultural (Tooby & Cosmides)
    - Our values today would shock those of our ancestors: (i) irreligion; (ii) disrespect families.
    - Value we put on work is contingent.

5. **We can choose new values.**
    - In the 1930s intellectuals talked about choosing new values.
    - It's delicate to talk about, respecting peoples' values, but acknoledging that they're contingent and aiming to change them.


#           The Workshop

The premise:

> "Is the dignity that work confers inseparable from the economic exchange currently at the heart of wage labor, or can it be grounded in other forms of contribution, service, and human relationship?

Two scenarios:

1. Scenario 1: The Post-Work Society. ("'what happens from a theological/faith perspective once wage labor disappears?")

    > "AI replaces almost all of labor while maximizing leisure time and quality of life. There are effective coordinated global redistribution efforts ensuring an adequate standard of living for everyone.

2. Scenario 2: The Great Pushback ("what if wage labor still exists, but we are subordinate to the machines?")

    > "Societies make the choice to preserve human jobs through professional licensing, human-in-the-loop requirements or job guarantee schemes. This shapes the development of AI as an increasingly powerful tool that augments workers.


#           Observations

We are discussing what would happen if the elevator stalls.
: 
    The scenarios seem .
    
    The scenarios were about worlds where work was mostly the same, but robots did it instead of humans. This doesn't seem very far off given our current state of technology, & in fact I think of this as the elevator stopping.
    
    If the elevator *does* keep going up, then the scenarios we are discussing would only be a transition phase. 

    If we build truly superhuman intelligence, what are the implications? The best analogy I can think of is a visitor from a future civilization, like a bronze-age civilization suddenly encountering a galleon from the machine-age. In this case we may have to worry about more things than the dignity of work.

A concrete vision: idling in apartment buildings.
: 
    It's useful to have a concrete idea of what this world looks like.

    - 7B people in apartment buildings; they can order anything off amazon, can get ideal medical service.
    - question: all gathering in communal areas, or plugged into a cable from the wall.


Values are mutable.
: 
    - Our brains & bodies were drawn towards hunter gathering for 200,000 years, then towards agriculture for 10,000 years, & industrial society for 150 years, & now the next thing.
    - Culture & values:
        - Rice-growing vs wheat-growing societies.
        - Religious beliefs all promote *quietism*, acceptance of the status quo. 
    - (evolutionary psychology: )

It's delicate to respect peoples' values but also see they're mutable.
: 
    Delicate task: respect peoples' values, but also recognize that their values are downstream of the economic circumstances.

We have some historical precedents for living post-scarcity.
: .
    - **Oil states / rentier states.**
        - In Kuwait 80% of the native population are employed in the public sector.
        - Classically rentier states are less accountable to the population.
    - **Religious minorities.**
        - Monks in Tibet & Mongolia were historically as high as 20-40% of males.
        - Israeli Haredi have 50% labor-force partricipation, compared to 85%.
    - **Long-term unemployed.**
        - 20% of young American men w/o education are outside the labor force, many w disability benefits.
        - There was a period of high long-term unemployment rates in Europe.
    - **Aristocrats / leisure class.**
    - **Retirees.** Most people used to work roughly until they died, now they typically have 20 years of retirement (median retirement at 62, at which point you have 20 years life expectancy)
    - **Social dividend.**
        - Alaska pays $1-2K/year.
        - Norway uses the money to fund social services.
    - **Hunter-gatherers.**
        - Sahlins (1966) "The Original Affluent Society", said they only worked 3-4 hours a day, though later debunked.


We can choose our values.
: 

    1. Nietzsche, 1880s, "revaluation of values"

    2. Lippman (1922) "Public Opinion".

    3. Keynes (1930) "Economic Possibilities for our Grandchildren" -- He said the emphasis on the virtue of hard work is old fashioned & we need to leave it behind, those virtues were specific to a certain period, & we'll have to learn to rise above them.

    4. Bertrand Russell (1932) "In Praise of Idleness"
    
        - > "Like most of my generation, I was brought up on the saying “Satan finds some mischief still for idle hands to do.”

        - > "a great deal of harm is being done in the modern world by the belief in the virtuousness of work, and that the road to happiness and prosperity lies in an organized diminution of work.

        - > "This is the morality of the Slave State, applied in circumstances totally unlike those in which it arose.

    5. Discourse in the 1930s was much more about active choice of values: Fascism, existentialism, propaganda. Post-war values we drew a cover over it. 

    6. Skinner (1971) "Beyond Freedom & Dignity"
    
        - No such thing as free will or dignity
        - *Social engineering*
    
        > “A scientific analysis of behavior must assume that a person’s behavior is controlled by his genetic and environmental histories rather than by the person himself as an initiating agent.”

        > “A person who has been controlled by positive reinforcement is said to deserve credit; the control is not recognized.”

        > “The problem is to arrange contingencies of reinforcement under which people behave in ways that will produce a better culture.”

    7. 18th century turn to capitalism
    
        - Hirschman, "The Passions and the Interests"
        - 18th century discourse: it's good to encourage people to follow their material interests, healthy diversion from status competition.


We instill values in our children.
: 
    We taech them to be stubborn, meticulous, polite, reliable, hard-working, disciplined.

    Notable difference in virtues middle-class and working-class values.
    
    My wife's cousins failed by being not concrete enough, my cousins failed by being too concrete.


What values to instill in Citlali?
: 
    1. Ability to enjoy things. Talk about sunsets, about music. (Like epicurians, aesthetes; Proust, Wilde, Bloomsbury circle, Ruskin.)
    2. Ability to float in a social environment. 

Awkwardness of our political philosophy.
: 
    We don't have a common political philosophy so these things are all hard to think about. As Alasdair Macintyre says: our principles are a mixtures of welfare-maximization, rights, virtues, traditions.

    Akwardness of pluralism: we say each person should pursue what they want, yet also:
    
    1. We understand that those values are determined by circumstances, so odd to think of them as sacrosanct;
    2. People don't think of their values as just like their favorite color, they think of them as having meaning outside themselves;
    3. We have strong opinions about the values of our children.

Presentism.
: 
    Surprising to me how everyone kept referring to us as existing in a fallen state. That society is already in a bad way. But not at all clear this is true.

Electric sheep dreaming of pasturse.
: 
    We went around the circle & talked about dream jobs: jazz musician, running a bookshop & cafe, ; eventually someone said hospice nurse and everyone politely clapped.


##          materials for workshop

> bring humanistic perspectives to the center of AI and work policy. The initiative has three core goals:
> • to elevate theological and humanistic perspectives in the national conversation about AI and work;
> • to build bipartisan momentum by grounding policy in values—dignity, faith, family, meaning—that resonate across the political spectrum;
> • and to generate new frameworks for thinking about the future of work that draw on the deepest wisdom of these traditions

--------------------------------------------

> Is the dignity that work confers inseparable from the economic exchange currently at the heart of wage labor, or can it be grounded in other forms of contribution, service, and human relationship?

--------------------------------------------


<!-- 
| Name                        | Affiliation / Employment                                             | Very brief précis                                                                       |
| --------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Adrian Brown                | Windfall Trust (CEO)                                                 | Economist/policy leader studying macroeconomic and social impacts of transformative AI. |
| Anna Yelizarova             | Windfall Trust (COO)                                                 | Works on economic resilience and policy responses to AI-driven disruption.              |
| Danny Buerkli               | Windfall Trust (Director of Product & Partnerships)                  | Builds partnerships and tools related to economic impacts of advanced AI.               |
| Yolanda Lannquist           | Windfall Trust (Global Governance Lead)                              | Works on international governance and policy coordination for transformative AI.        |
|                             |                                                                      |                                                                                         |
| Adam Kronk                  | Institute for Ethics and the Common Good, University of Notre Dame   | Leadership scholar directing a network on ethical leadership and organizational values. |
| Angie Appleby Purcell       | Institute for Ethics and the Common Good, University of Notre Dame   | Managing director overseeing strategy and operations for Notre Dame’s ethics institute. |
| Meghan Sullivan             | University of Notre Dame (Philosophy; Notre Dame Ethics Initiative)  | Moral philosopher working on ethics, philosophy of religion, and AI ethics.             |
| Julia French                | DELTA Network, Institute for Ethics and the Common Good, Notre Dame  | Coordinates collaborations and programming on ethics, technology, and leadership.       |
| Yong Lee                    | Notre Dame                                                           |                                                                                         |
|                             |                                                                      |                                                                                         |
| Brian Patrick Green         | Markkula Center for Applied Ethics, Santa Clara University           | Technology ethicist focused on AI, space, and emerging-technology governance.           |
| Christopher Wong Michaelson | University of St. Thomas (Opus College of Business)                  | Philosopher researching meaningful work and business ethics.                            |
| Claudia Strauss             | Pitzer College                                                       | Anthropologist studying cultural models and public narratives about work and society.   |
| David Evan Harris           | University of California, Berkeley (AI policy & governance research) | Scholar and policy advisor working on responsible AI governance and public policy.      |
| Kate Ward                   | Marquette University                                                 | Theological ethicist studying work, technology, and Catholic social thought.            |
| Michael Wear                | Center for Christianity and Public Life (Founder & CEO)              | Public-policy leader connecting religious thought with civic and political life.        |
| Sara Wolkenfeld             | Sefaria (Chief Learning Officer)                                     | Scholar of Jewish texts working on digital access to religious scholarship.             |
|                             |                                                                      |                                                                                         |
| Molly Kinder                | Brookings Institution                                                | Labor economist researching AI’s effects on jobs and labor markets.                     |
|                             |                                                                      |                                                                                         |
| Stephanie Chan              | Google DeepMind                                                      | Researcher studying societal impacts and governance of advanced AI systems.             |
| Josh Achiam                 | OpenAI                                                               |                                                                                         |
| David Robinson              | OpenAI                                                               |                                                                                         |
| Chloe Lubinski              | Anthropic (Interpretability Research)                                | Researcher studying internal mechanisms and interpretability of large AI models.        |
| Saffron Huang               |                                                                      |                                                                                         |
| Tom Cunningham              | METR                                                                 |                                                                                         | -->


--------------------------------------------
